title: King and Pawn vs King
summary: The most common endgame — master it

steps.title: The Core Endgame
steps.text:
## K + P vs K — every player must know it

More games are decided by **king and pawn vs king** than by any other technical ending. It is the **gateway endgame** — every rook, queen, or minor-piece ending eventually simplifies into one of its patterns.

### What this endgame looks like

- One side has **king + a single pawn**.
- The other has only the **king**.
- A central pawn (b through g file) — rook pawns are a separate special case.

### The key concept: get in front with opposition

The entire result hinges on one question: **can the stronger side place his king IN FRONT of the pawn while holding the opposition?** If yes — the pawn promotes. If not — it is a draw by stalemate or by the defending king reaching the queening square.

### Procedure to convert

1. **Lead with the king**, never the pawn.
2. Reach a **key square** (see next page) with the king.
3. **Take the opposition**, then outflank diagonally.
4. Push the pawn only when the king has cleared the lane.
5. Promote and switch to the K+Q mate.

### Common mistakes

- Pushing the pawn first — it is captured or blockaded.
- Letting the enemy king reach the **queening square** — instant draw.
- Allowing **stalemate** with the pawn on the 7th rank and your king right behind it on the 6th in front of him.

> King in front, pawn behind.

steps.title: Key Squares
steps.text:
## Key squares win this ending

For a pawn on the **2nd–5th rank**, the **key squares** are the three squares **two ranks ahead** of the pawn. For our pawn on **e4**, the keys are **d6, e6, f6**.

For a pawn on the **6th rank**, the key squares are simply the three squares **directly in front** of it.

### The rule

If your king reaches **any key square**, you win — *regardless of opposition*. That is what makes them "key."

### Procedure with this position

White king is **already on e6** (a key square!). The win is now mechanical:

1. {{Kd6}} or {{Kf6}} — outflank to grab a second key square with tempo.
2. After Black retreats, push {{e5}}.
3. March the king ahead of the pawn until promotion.

### Common mistakes

- Stepping the king to **e7** — it leaves the pawn unprotected and walks into stalemate motifs.
- Pushing the pawn while the king is on a key square — you give the squares back.
- Treating rook-pawn (a/h) endings the same way — they are usually drawn even when the technique looks similar.

### Model line

{{Kd6}} Kf7 {{e5}} Ke8 {{Kc7}} and the pawn promotes.

> Reach a key square with the move and the pawn queens itself.

steps.prompt: Step to a **second key square** to outflank the defender. The principle: *king first, pawn later*.
steps.hint: Two key squares are one diagonal step away — pick either flank.
